metadata.title: Clinical Investigation of Corticosteroids Intended for Use on the Skin

rules.rule_text: Above a certain concentration in a given vehicle, further increases in corticosteroid concentration do not produce proportionately greater therapeutic effect but do increase adverse reactions. Optimal concentration must be determined by comparative investigation.
rules.verbatim_quote: above a certain concentration in a given vehicle, a further increase in concentration does not result in a proportionately greater effect but does increase the occurrence of adverse reactions

rules.rule_text: The influence of factors affecting penetration and local activity (concentration, formulation, site of application, skin condition, conditions of application including occlusion) must be taken into account during clinical trials.
rules.verbatim_quote: The influence of these different factors should be taken into account during the clinical trials.

rules.rule_text: Four levels of activity must be distinguished for topical corticosteroids: mild, moderately strong, strong, and very strong. The classification depends on the substance, concentration, and vehicle.
rules.verbatim_quote: it is customary to distinguish, depending on the substance and the concentration, four levels of activity ranging from mild via moderately strong and strong to very strong.
rules.exceptions: The vehicle can shift the activity classification to an adjacent level

rules.rule_text: Indications for topical corticosteroids are directly linked to the level of activity. Very strong activity products are indicated for localised and resistant plaques (psoriasis, lichenification, discoid lupus, lichen hypertrophic) and hypertrophic scars.
rules.verbatim_quote: It is generally accepted that some indications of corticosensitive dermatosis are directly linked to the level of activity of the preparation as reported in Tables I and II annexed
rules.exceptions: List is for guidance and may be adapted to national practices

rules.rule_text: The primary safety concern for topical corticosteroids under normal conditions of use is irreversible local adverse reactions (particularly dermatrophia), not systemic effects from percutaneous resorption.
rules.verbatim_quote: it is not the possible systemic adverse effects due to percutaneous resorption which are the first concern, but the often irreversible local adverse reactions on the skin, such as dermatrophia.

rules.rule_text: Local adverse effects to monitor include: skin atrophy (clinical thinning, tele-angiectasia, purpura, striae), rosacea-like and perioral dermatitis, rebound leading to steroid dependence, impairment of healing, ocular effects (glaucoma, cataract, mycosis, herpes simplex), depigmentation, and hypertrichosis.
rules.verbatim_quote: skin atrophy which becomes often irreversible producing clinical thinning of the skin, tele-angiectasia, purpura, striae; b) rosacea-like and perioral dermatitis with or without skin atrophy; c) rebound which may lead to steroid 'dependence'; d) impairment of healing; e) effects on the eye: increased risk of glaucoma, cataract, exacerbation of mycosis and of herpes simplex; f) miscellaneous: depigmentation, hypertrichosis, etc.

rules.rule_text: Systemic effects in children from topical corticosteroids occur more readily than in adults and include acute adrenal insufficiency, hypercorticism, arrest of growth, and intracranial hypertension, due to high surface/weight ratio and unintentional occlusion by napkins.
rules.verbatim_quote: They occur more readily in children (e.g. acute adrenal insufficiency at the discontinuation of the substance, hypercorticism, arrest of growth, intracranial hypertension) because of the high surface/weight ratio and unintentional occlusion by napkins.

rules.rule_text: Vasoconstriction tests (McKenzie and Stoughton method) must be performed to compare the new corticosteroid product with established preparations of approximately equal, stronger, and weaker activity to position it in the spectrum of corticosteroid products.
rules.verbatim_quote: Vasoconstriction tests derived from McKenzie and Stoughton (Archives of Dermatology, 1962, 86, 608-10), using the new substance incorporated in its base should therefore be compared with the effects of established corticosteroid preparations, not only with those of approximately equal activity, but also with stronger and weaker preparations.
rules.exceptions: Other methods may be used; regulatory agencies will consider new methods if clinical relevance is demonstrated

rules.rule_text: The level of activity estimated from pharmacodynamic tests must always be confirmed by clinical investigation.
rules.verbatim_quote: The level of activity estimated in the light of these tests will always have to be confirmed by clinical investigation (see 3.2.1).

rules.rule_text: Clinical efficacy studies must use randomised double-blind design. Single-blind investigation is permitted only if double-blind is virtually impossible. Parallel groups are probably superior but intra-subject half-side (left-right) comparisons are also acceptable.
rules.verbatim_quote: A product should be studied in randomised double-blind investigations; single-blind investigation should only be permitted if it is virtually impossible to perform double-blind tests. Parallel groups are probably superior in most instances, but there have been conflicting opinions expressed about whether parallel groups are preferable to intra subject half-side studies (left-right comparisons).
rules.exceptions: For left-right studies, attention must be paid to systemic transfer between sides, especially when comparing products of different activities on large areas

rules.rule_text: A topical corticosteroid product must be studied against its base and against known products of different activity. Where the product is a minor modification of an existing one, comparison with the reference product via vasoconstriction test may suffice.
rules.verbatim_quote: A product should be studied in principle against its base but also against known products of different activity. Where the product can be regarded as a minor modification of an existing one, it will often be possible to rely on a comparison with this latter product used as a reference, at least through an adequate vasoconstriction test.
rules.exceptions: Minor modifications may rely on vasoconstriction test comparison alone

rules.rule_text: For new corticosteroids, the optimal concentration for clinical use must be confirmed by comparative investigation of various concentrations, because above a certain concentration adverse effects increase without notable improvement in therapeutic efficacy.
rules.verbatim_quote: For new corticosteroids the optimal concentration for clinical use will have to be confirmed by comparative investigation of various concentrations. This is important because of the possibility of increase in the occurrence of adverse effects above a certain concentration... without notable improvement of therapeutic efficacy.

rules.rule_text: Clinical trial reports must clearly state frequency of application, sites of application, areas treated, and amounts of product used per week. Application frequency of once or at most twice daily is usual; deviations must be justified.
rules.verbatim_quote: Reports should give a clear picture of the frequency of application, especially in the initial period of treatment. When frequencies differ from those usually recommended (i.e. once or at most twice daily), it is desirable that this should be justified by the applicant. Reports should also clearly state the sites of application, the areas which have been treated and the amounts of product used per week.

rules.rule_text: If a preparation is intended for use under occlusion, the influence of occlusion on therapeutic effect must be examined in clinical studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: If the preparation is intended to be used under occlusion, the influence of this on the effect in clinical studies should be examined.

rules.rule_text: Valid comparisons of topical corticosteroids can only be made between treatments given for the same condition. The product must be tested in a series of conditions requiring different degrees of treatment intensity.
rules.verbatim_quote: Valid comparisons can only be made between treatments given for the same condition. A product should be tested in a series of conditions, each requiring different degrees of intensity of treatment.

rules.rule_text: Psoriasis and atopic dermatitis are the most suitable conditions for clinical testing of topical corticosteroids. Psoriasis is advisable for very strong and strong products; atopic dermatitis for moderately strong and mild products.
rules.verbatim_quote: psoriasis and atopic dermatitis, respectively, being the most suitable conditions for testing.
rules.exceptions: Other indications may be suggested if positive results obtained in specific clinical studies with adequate methodology

rules.rule_text: It is not acceptable to assume that a substance active at a certain dose level will be equally suitable at other levels for more-severe or less-severe disease. This must be tested.
rules.verbatim_quote: As a general principle it is not satisfactory to assume that a substance active at a certain level of dose will be equally suitable at other levels for more-severe or less-severe disease.

rules.rule_text: Short-term studies of one or two weeks may not be sufficient for clinical comparison. Long-term studies of efficacy and safety (e.g., over 3 months) may be necessary depending on the novelty of the product and indications claimed.
rules.verbatim_quote: Short term studies over one or two weeks may not be the only relevant investigation for the clinical comparison of two topical steroids... certain studies of efficacy as well as of safety in long-term use (e.g. over a period of three months) will sometimes be necessary.
rules.exceptions: Depends on novelty of the product and indications claimed

rules.rule_text: Claims of fewer or less severe adverse effects compared to established substances of the same activity class must be justified by controlled studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: Where a product is claimed to produce less frequent or severe adverse effects than a well-established substance of the same degree of activity, this should be justified by controlled studies.

rules.rule_text: Atrophogenic activity must be determined: the degree of atrophy induced must be measured after application, specifying sites and mode of use. Epidermal thinning and dermal alteration must be detected using methods such as radiography, ultrasonography, histology, collagen fibril measurement, or stereomicroscopy. These changes are undetectable until at least 4 weeks of normal use.
rules.verbatim_quote: The degree of atrophy induced by the product will have to be determined, after application of the product. The mode of use including the sites of application should be specified. The changes due to thinning of the epidermis and alteration of the dermis can be detected by different methods, such as measurement of the thickness of the skin (radiographical techniques, ultrasonography), histology, measurement of collagen fibrils, stereomicroscopy. They cannot be detected until a topical treatment has been given under normal conditions of use for four weeks.
rules.exceptions: Under experimental conditions with occlusion, exposure period can be shorter

rules.rule_text: Tolerance must be determined (skin drying, irritation, sensitisation) for both the complete product and the base. Post-trial patch tests must be performed with the pharmaceutical formulation and the base.
rules.verbatim_quote: In any study, tolerance will have to be determined (drying of skin, irritation, sensitisation) both for the product as a whole and for the base. After conclusion of the trial, patch tests should be performed with the pharmaceutical formulation and the base.

rules.rule_text: HPA axis suppression must be assessed via plasma cortisol determinations (preferred over urinary cortisol metabolites). Plasma cortisol must be measured accounting for diurnal rhythm, stress, sex, and seasonal influences, at: day 5 and day 20 without occlusion, day 5 with occlusion on normal and diseased skin, and several times in patients treated for long periods. ACTH stimulation testing is also recommended.
rules.verbatim_quote: plasma cortisol determinations give more information than studies of cortisol metabolites in urine. When performing plasma cortisol determinations account must be taken of the diurnal rhythm exhibited by plasma cortisol levels and of factors which influence these levels, such as stress, sex, seasonal influences, use of oral contraceptives etc... it is desirable to determine plasma cortisol levels under such conditions as: at the fifth and at the 20th day of treatment without occlusion and at the fifth day with occlusion on the normal and diseased skin, several times, in a number of patients treated for long periods.
